criteria: Tokenizer recognizes SQL keywords (SELECT, INSERT, CREATE, WHERE, JOIN, etc.) case-insensitively
String literals enclosed in single quotes are parsed including escaped quotes ('it''s' → it's)
Numeric literals including integers and floating-point values (42, 3.14, -7) are recognized as distinct token types
Operators (=, <, >, <=, >=, !=, <>) and punctuation (comma, parentheses, semicolon) are tokenized as distinct tokens
Identifiers (table names, column names) support quoted identifiers with double quotes ("column name")
Tokenizer reports error position (line and column) for unrecognized characters
Tokenizer returns a list or stream of objects containing type, value, line, and column
Test suite of 20+ diverse SQL statements produces the expected token sequence
Tokenizer handles both line comments (--) and block comments (/* */)
Tokenizer correctly distinguishes between identifiers and keywords via case-insensitive lookup
SELECT parser produces AST with column list, FROM clause, optional WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT
INSERT parser produces AST with target table, optional column names, and VALUES clause with multiple rows
CREATE TABLE parser extracts table name and column definitions with types (INTEGER, TEXT, REAL, BLOB) and constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
Expression parser correctly handles operator precedence: NOT > comparison (=,<,>,<=,>=,<>) > AND > OR
Parenthesized expressions correctly override default precedence levels
NULL keyword is parsed as LiteralExpression (not IdentifierExpression)
String and numeric literals are parsed as LiteralExpression nodes
Column references are parsed as IdentifierExpression nodes
Binary expressions (AND, OR, comparison operators) produce BinaryExpression AST nodes
Unary NOT expression produces UnaryExpression AST node
Parser provides error position (line and column) for syntax errors
Test suite covers at least 15 valid SQL statements across SELECT, INSERT, and CREATE TABLE
Test suite correctly rejects at least 10 invalid SQL statements with position information
Tokenizer output correctly consumed as parser input (token stream integration)
SELECT * wildcard correctly produces AST with is_wildcard flag set
ORDER BY correctly captures ASC/DESC direction
LIMIT clause correctly parses numeric literal expression
Compiler translates SELECT AST into a bytecode program with opcodes for OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, and Halt
Compiler translates INSERT AST into bytecode with opcodes for OpenTable, MakeRecord, Insert, and Halt
Virtual machine executes bytecode programs step-by-step, processing one opcode per cycle in a fetch-decode-execute loop
VM maintains a register file (array of typed values) for intermediate computation with at least 256 registers
EXPLAIN command outputs the bytecode program for a given SQL statement in human-readable format showing opcode, operands, and targets
WHERE clause compiles to conditional jump opcodes (e.g., Gt, Le, Ne) that skip non-matching rows during execution
Expression compilation uses register allocation to assign each sub-expression to a register
Bytecode execution of 'SELECT * FROM t' on a 10,000-row table completes in under 100ms
Compiler generates linear bytecode sequence from tree-structured AST without recursive tree-walking at execution time
Cursor abstraction layer decouples VDBE from storage engine implementation
Binary operators (AND, OR, comparison operators) compile to dedicated opcodes that operate on registers
Literal values (integers, strings) compile to immediate-load opcodes that populate registers
Column references compile to OP_COLUMN instructions that load from cursor to register
Buffer pool manages a configurable number of in-memory page frames (default 1000 pages)
Pages are fixed-size (4096 bytes by default, configurable)
FetchPage loads a page from disk into a free frame, or returns the cached frame if already resident
LRU eviction selects the least recently used unpinned page for replacement when no free frames exist
Dirty page tracking marks pages modified in memory; eviction writes dirty pages to disk before replacement
Pin/Unpin mechanism prevents eviction of pages currently in use by B-tree operations
FlushAll writes all dirty pages to disk (used before checkpoint or shutdown)
Buffer pool hit rate is measurable and logged for performance tuning
Buffer pool initializes with a fixed number of 4096-byte frames
FetchPage returns the correct page from memory if already loaded (hit)
FetchPage loads page from disk if not in memory (miss)
LRU algorithm correctly identifies the least recently used page for eviction
Pinned pages (count > 0) are never selected for eviction
Dirty pages are written back to disk only when evicted or on FlushAll
Buffer pool hit rate is tracked and accessible for performance metrics
Page header contains page type (leaf/internal, table/index), cell count, free space pointer, and right-child pointer (internal only)
Table B-tree leaf pages store rows keyed by rowid with variable-length record encoding
Table B-tree internal pages store rowid separator keys and child page numbers
Index B+tree leaf pages store (indexed column value, rowid) pairs with data only in leaves
Index B+tree internal pages store only separator keys and child pointers (no row data)
CREATE TABLE creates a B-tree root page and records the schema in a system catalog (sqlite_master equivalent)
INSERT serializes a row and inserts into the correct B-tree leaf; node splitting creates a new page and promotes a separator key
Full table scan traverses all leaf pages in rowid order, returning all rows
Pages serialize to and deserialize from exactly 4096-byte buffers via the buffer pool
Page header correctly identifies Leaf vs Internal and Table vs Index types using bit encoding (0x0D/0x05 for tables, 0x0B/0x0A for indexes)
Slotted page format implements bidirectional growth (cell pointers at end grow upward, cell content at start grows downward)
Table B-tree stores full records in leaf nodes keyed by rowid
Index B+tree stores key/rowid pairs only in leaf nodes
Node split algorithm correctly rebalances the tree by moving roughly half cells to new page and promoting separator key to parent
Varint implementation handles 1-9 byte encoding for 64-bit integers using continuation bits
System catalog (sqlite_master) persists table root page numbers and column definitions
Full table scan successfully iterates through all leaf pages in order using B-tree traversal
SELECT * FROM table returns all rows in rowid order via B-tree leaf scan
SELECT col1, col2 returns only specified columns (projection)
WHERE clause filters rows during scan, evaluating boolean expressions on deserialized column values
INSERT adds a row to the B-tree; subsequent SELECT returns the inserted data
UPDATE modifies columns in rows matching WHERE; subsequent SELECT reflects changes
DELETE removes rows matching WHERE; subsequent SELECT no longer returns them
NOT NULL constraint rejects INSERT or UPDATE setting a NOT NULL column to null
Operations on non-existent tables return an error with the table name
SELECT * returns all rows by iterating through the B-tree leaf sequence
SELECT with column names correctly projects only the requested fields
WHERE clause correctly filters rows using Three-Valued Logic (handling NULLs)
INSERT adds a new row and updates the B-tree structure correctly
UPDATE and DELETE modify/remove rows while maintaining B-tree integrity
NOT NULL constraints reject invalid writes with a descriptive error
Attempting to query a table not in the System Catalog returns an 'undefined table' error
WHERE clause correctly filters rows using Three-Valued Logic (NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL, not TRUE)
Column projection correctly deserializes variable-length records to extract requested fields
INSERT with NULL for INTEGER PRIMARY KEY triggers auto-increment behavior
UPDATE cannot change the rowid (primary key) - must reject or handle as delete+insert
DELETE during iteration uses two-pass approach to avoid cursor corruption
CREATE INDEX builds a B+tree index mapping (indexed column value → rowid) from existing table data
Index is automatically maintained on INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE (index entries added/removed/updated)
Index lookup retrieves rows matching an equality predicate without full table scan, verified by counting pages read
Range scan on index returns rows within a value range using B+tree leaf traversal
Query execution uses index scan when an indexed column appears in WHERE with equality or range predicate
UNIQUE index rejects INSERT or UPDATE that would create duplicate values
CREATE INDEX builds a B+tree mapping column values to rowids
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations maintain all associated indexes synchronously
Index lookup (equality) avoids full table scan and visits significantly fewer pages
Index range scan (BETWEEN or < >) traverses linked leaf pages
UNIQUE index correctly rejects duplicate value insertions
Bytecode VM can perform a Double Lookup from index cursor to table cursor
Composite index follows leftmost prefix rule
Covering index enables index-only scans when all needed columns are in the index
Index selectivity affects optimizer decision between index scan and table scan
ANALYZE command collects row count per table and stores in statistics catalog
ANALYZE command collects distinct value count (n_distinct) per indexed column
Cost model calculates table scan cost as total_pages × sequential_read_cost + row_count × cpu_cost
Cost model calculates index scan cost as index_pages × random_read_cost + matched_rows × random_read_cost
Planner estimates selectivity for equality (=) as 1/n_distinct
Planner estimates selectivity for range operators (<, >, <=, >=) using default assumption (~25%)
Planner selects index scan when index_cost < table_scan_cost
Planner applies selectivity threshold (~20%) to decide between index and table scan
PLANNER falls back to table scan when no suitable index exists
EXPLAIN outputs human-readable plan showing scan type (TABLE SCAN or USING INDEX)
EXPLAIN shows index name when index is used
EXPLAIN shows estimated row count and selectivity percentage
Join planner estimates cardinality for each join pair using table statistics
Join planner uses dynamic programming or heuristic to find optimal join order
Without ANALYZE, planner uses default row count assumption (1M rows)
Statistics become stale after significant data changes; re-ANALYZE needed
BEGIN starts a transaction; all subsequent writes are buffered until COMMIT or ROLLBACK
COMMIT makes all changes permanent by flushing dirty pages and removing the rollback journal
ROLLBACK undoes all changes by restoring original pages from the rollback journal
Rollback journal records original page contents BEFORE modification (for undo on crash)
Changes are not visible to other connections until COMMIT (basic read isolation)
Crash recovery on startup detects an existing rollback journal and automatically rolls back the incomplete transaction
Journal file is fsync'd before modified pages are written to the database file (write ordering guarantee)
BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK commands correctly toggle the engine state
A .db-journal file is created and contains original page data before any write to the main .db file
The journal file is physically flushed to disk (fsync) before the main database is modified
A manual ROLLBACK restores the state from the journal and clears the journal file
Startup logic detects a Hot Journal and automatically restores the database to a consistent state
Writes are not visible to other database connections until the COMMIT is complete
BEGIN starts a transaction and buffers all subsequent writes until COMMIT or ROLLBACK
Rollback journal records original page contents BEFORE any modification to the database file
Changes are not visible to other database connections until COMMIT completes
BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK commands correctly toggle the transaction manager state
A manual ROLLBACK restores the database state from the journal and deletes the journal file
Journal contains a commit marker to distinguish committed vs rolled-back transactions
Recovery procedure correctly interprets journal state (commit marker present = committed, absent = rollback)
Transaction state machine correctly tracks NONE → ACTIVE → COMMITTING/ROLLING_BACK → terminal states
WAL mode appends modified pages to a separate WAL file instead of modifying the main database file
Writers append to WAL; readers check WAL for the most recent version of a page before reading from the main database
Multiple readers can execute queries concurrently while a single writer appends to the WAL without blocking
Checkpoint (PRAGMA wal_checkpoint) copies WAL pages back into the main database file
WAL checkpoint is required to prevent unbounded WAL growth—auto-checkpoint triggers after configurable page count (default 1000)
Readers see a consistent snapshot: a reader that starts before a commit does not see that commit's changes (snapshot isolation)
WAL file corruption is detected via page checksums—frames with invalid checksums are rejected
Writers append to a separate WAL file instead of modifying the main .db file
Readers search the WAL for the most recent page version before falling back to the main file
Writers and multiple readers can operate simultaneously without blocking
Checkpointing copies WAL pages to the main database and truncates the WAL
Automatic checkpoint triggers after 1000 pages (configurable)
Readers use a consistent snapshot based on the WAL state at their start time
Checksums are used to detect and reject corrupted WAL frames
WAL commit is faster than rollback journal commit due to sequential appends instead of random writes
COUNT(*) returns accurate count including NULL rows
COUNT(column) ignores NULL values in target column
SUM produces correct sum over grouped and ungrouped data
AVG returns floating-point result even for INTEGER columns
AVG correctly ignores NULL values during computation
MIN returns minimum non-NULL value in column
MAX returns maximum non-NULL value in column
GROUP BY partitions rows into groups before aggregation
GROUP BY without ORDER BY may return groups in undefined order
HAVING filters groups after aggregate computation based on aggregate values
INNER JOIN combines rows from two tables using nested loop algorithm
JOIN with WHERE clause correctly filters rows before or during join execution
Empty table with aggregates returns 0 for COUNT, NULL for SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX
Multiple aggregates in single query (COUNT, SUM, AVG) all compute correctly
NULL values are correctly ignored in aggregate functions (except COUNT(*))

criteria.description: SELECT parser produces AST with column list, FROM, and optional WHERE/LIMIT
criteria.test: Parse 'SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE age >= 18' - verify all clause nodes present

criteria.description: INSERT parser handles target table and VALUES mapping
criteria.test: Parse 'INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ("Bob", 30)' - verify table name and values parsed

criteria.description: CREATE TABLE parser extracts column names, types, and constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL)
criteria.test: Parse 'CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL)' - verify constraints extracted

criteria.description: Expression parser correctly handles NOT > AND > OR precedence
criteria.test: Parse 'a AND b OR c' - verify AST groups as '(a AND b) OR c'

criteria.description: Parenthesized expressions correctly override default precedence levels
criteria.test: Parse 'a AND (b OR c)' - verify AST groups as 'a AND (b OR c)'

criteria.description: Parser provides error position (line and column) for syntax errors
criteria.test: Parse 'SELECT FROM users' - verify error at column 8 (after SELECT)

criteria.description: Parser reports meaningful error messages for unexpected tokens
criteria.test: Parse 'SELECT * FROM' - verify error message contains 'expected table name'

criteria.description: NULL keyword is parsed as LiteralExpression not IdentifierExpression
criteria.test: Parse 'SELECT NULL' - verify NULL is NODE_LITERAL not NODE_COLUMN_REF

criteria.description: Test suite passes for 15+ valid SQL statements across SELECT, INSERT, and CREATE TABLE
criteria.test: Run test_valid_statements() - all 15+ cases parse without error

criteria.description: Test suite correctly rejects 10+ invalid SQL statements with position information
criteria.test: Run test_invalid_statements() - all 10+ cases report errors with positions

criteria.description: Compiler translates SELECT AST into a bytecode program with opcodes for OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, Halt
criteria.test: Compile 'SELECT * FROM t' and verify presence of OpenRead, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, Close, Halt opcodes in sequence

criteria.description: Compiler translates INSERT AST into bytecode with opcodes for OpenTable, MakeRecord, Insert, Halt
criteria.test: Compile 'INSERT INTO t VALUES (1)' and verify MakeRecord and Insert opcodes

criteria.description: Virtual machine executes bytecode programs step-by-step, processing one opcode per cycle
criteria.test: Execute compiled program and verify each opcode is executed in sequence

criteria.description: VM maintains a register file (array of typed values) for intermediate computation
criteria.test: Execute expression '1 + 2' and verify registers store intermediate values correctly

criteria.description: EXPLAIN command outputs the bytecode program for a given SQL statement in human-readable format
criteria.test: Run EXPLAIN on query and verify output shows addr, opcode, p1, p2, p3 columns

criteria.description: WHERE clause compiles to conditional jump opcodes that skip non-matching rows
criteria.test: Compile 'SELECT * FROM t WHERE x = 1' and verify IF_NOT opcode targets skip location

criteria.description: Bytecode execution of 'SELECT * FROM t' on a 10,000-row table completes in under 100ms
criteria.test: Execute compiled bytecode against test table with 10K rows and measure time

criteria.description: Compiler translates SELECT AST into opcodes including OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, and Halt
criteria.test: Verify complete opcode sequence for SELECT

criteria.description: VM executes bytecode in a fetch-decode-execute loop, processing one opcode per cycle
criteria.test: Verify VM processes each instruction sequentially

criteria.description: The EXPLAIN command displays the human-readable opcode sequence for any valid SQL statement
criteria.test: EXPLAIN outputs readable bytecode for SELECT, INSERT, CREATE TABLE

criteria.description: Buffer pool manages a configurable number of in-memory page frames (default 1000 pages)
Pages are fixed-size (4096 bytes by default, configurable)
FetchPage loads a page from disk into a free frame, or returns the cached frame if already resident
LRU eviction selects the least recently used unpinned page for replacement when no free frames exist
Dirty page tracking marks pages modified in memory; eviction writes dirty pages to disk before replacement
Pin/Unpin mechanism prevents eviction of pages currently in use by B-tree operations
FlushAll writes all dirty pages to disk (used before checkpoint or shutdown)
Buffer pool hit rate is measurable and logged for performance tuning
Buffer pool initializes with a fixed number of 4096-byte frames
FetchPage returns the correct page from memory if already loaded (hit)
FetchPage loads page from disk if not in memory (miss)
LRU algorithm correctly identifies the least recently used page for eviction
Pinned pages (count > 0) are never selected for eviction
Dirty pages are written back to disk only when evicted or on FlushAll
Buffer pool hit rate is tracked and accessible for performance metrics
VDBE executes bytecode program to completion without crashes
SELECT returns correct rows matching WHERE clause
criteria.verification: Run SELECT with various WHERE conditions, verify results

criteria.description: Three-valued logic correctly evaluates NULL comparisons
criteria.verification: Test IS NULL, IS NOT NULL, = NULL, <> NULL with actual NULLs

criteria.description: INSERT adds valid row to table and returns rowid
criteria.verification: Insert row, query to verify it exists

criteria.description: UPDATE modifies existing rows with correct filtering
DELETE removes rows from table correctly
NOT NULL constraint enforced on INSERT/UPDATE
criteria.verification: Attempt INSERT NULL into NOT NULL column, expect error

criteria.description: Column projection returns correct columns for SELECT
criteria.verification: SELECT specific columns, verify only those returned

criteria.description: Row serialization/deserialization maintains data integrity
criteria.verification: Insert various data types, read back, verify exact match

criteria.description: Cursor properly traverses table in rowid order
criteria.verification: SELECT * without ORDER BY, verify rowid sequence

criteria.description: ANALYZE command collects and stores table/index statistics
criteria.verification: Run ANALYZE on test table, verify sqlite_stat1/4 populated with row counts and histograms

criteria.description: Selectivity estimation returns 0.0-1.0 for WHERE clauses
criteria.verification: Test equality, range, LIKE, NULL predicates; verify AND/OR combination formulas

criteria.description: Cost model prefers index when selectivity < 20%
criteria.verification: Compare costs for index vs table scan; verify index chosen for selective queries

criteria.description: Query planner generates ExecutionPlan with chosen access paths
criteria.verification: Create plans for various query patterns; verify correct access method selection

criteria.description: EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN shows human-readable access method
criteria.verification: Run EXPLAIN on queries; verify SCAN vs SEARCH USING INDEX in output

criteria.description: Join optimizer produces efficient join order
criteria.verification: Multi-table join queries; verify smaller table drives join

criteria.description: Fallback to defaults when statistics missing
criteria.verification: Query tables without ANALYZE; verify query still executes with defaults

criteria.description: Statistics marked stale after data modification
criteria.verification: ANALYZE, insert 20% more data, verify statistics_is_stale() returns true

criteria.description: BEGIN starts a transaction with unique ID
COMMIT persists all changes atomically
ROLLBACK reverts all changes to pre-transaction state
Journal captures original page images before modification
fsync occurs on journal before any database write
Crash recovery detects committed vs. uncommitted transactions
Recovery restores correct database state after crash
Torn pages are detected and handled correctly
Savepoints enable nested transaction control
Autocommit handles single-statement transactions
Write failures trigger automatic rollback
fsync failures prevent corrupt committed state
Corrupt journal frames are skipped during recovery
Invalid state transitions return appropriate errors
Journal throughput >= 50 MB/s on test hardware
Commit latency <= 50 ms for 10-page transaction
Recovery time <= 500 ms for 100-page transaction
Unit tests cover all state transitions
Unit tests cover journal format validation
Integration tests cover commit-then-crash scenario
Integration tests cover rollback-then-crash scenario
Integration tests cover SQL command flow
Edge case: empty transaction (BEGIN; COMMIT;)
Edge case: zero-page journal (single DELETE)
